questions.question: According to research, what percentage of people choose mentors/guides based on perceived authenticity and credibility rather than credentials alone?

options.text: About 45% choose based on authenticity
About 62% choose based on authenticity
About 84% choose based on authenticity and credibility
Nearly all (95%+) require formal credentials

questions.explanation: Research suggests 84% of people choose mentors/guides based on perceived authenticity and credibility rather than credentials alone. Additionally, authentic brands generate 3.5x greater engagement than overly-polished ones. This means your experiential wisdom and genuine voice matter more than degrees or titles—though credibility still needs to be established through transparency, quality work, social proof, and consistency.

questions.question: What is the difference between self-promotion and authentic branding?

options.text: They are the same thing—all branding is self-promotion
Self-promotion focuses on yourself and status; authentic branding focuses on how you serve others and what value you offer
Self-promotion is honest; authentic branding is fake
There is no such thing as authentic branding—all marketing is manipulation

questions.explanation: Self-promotion focuses on yourself, your achievements, how impressive you are—motivated by validation, status, ego. It feels like 'Look at me!' Authentic branding focuses on how you serve others, what value you offer, who you help—motivated by connection, service, fit. It feels like 'Here's how I might help...' Personal brand simply means how you're known and what people can expect from you—it's your reputation and whether it helps the right people find you.

questions.question: How long does it typically take to build genuine credibility according to the lesson?

options.text: 3-6 months of consistent work
About 1 year
2-5 years of consistent quality work
Credibility comes instantly from viral moments

questions.explanation: Building genuine credibility takes 2-5 years of consistent quality work. It accumulates through showing up consistently—not viral moments or shortcuts. People trust those who are still there in Year 3, not just the launch hype. Credibility is built through: transparency about expertise source, demonstrating depth through content quality, social proof/testimonials, and consistency over time.

questions.question: Why is specificity about who you serve important in personal branding?

options.text: Specificity limits your potential audience and should be avoided
Trying to serve everyone means serving no one deeply—specificity attracts aligned people and repels poor fits (both healthy outcomes)
Specificity only matters if you have formal credentials
You should be vague to appeal to the widest possible audience

questions.explanation: Specificity is clarifying, not limiting. If you try to serve everyone, you serve no one deeply. Better to be meaningful to 100 aligned people than vaguely interesting to 10,000 who aren't serious. Specificity repels some people—that's GOOD! It attracts those who truly resonate while helping others self-select out. Both outcomes are healthy. Example: 'I help women navigating empty nest transitions' is more effective than 'I help people with life transitions.'

questions.question: When does personal branding become unhealthy and require stepping back?

options.text: Whenever you create any public-facing content or have a social media presence
When you obsessively check metrics as validation, feel worthless when metrics drop, or make decisions based on attention rather than service
When you clearly state who you serve and who you don't serve
When you build consistency over time rather than seeking viral moments

questions.explanation: Branding becomes unhealthy when: obsessively checking follower counts/likes for validation, feeling worthless when metrics drop, constantly comparing yourself to others, making decisions based on what gets attention rather than what serves learners, or crafting false persona. These indicate ego-driven or performative branding. Reconnect with your WHY (who you serve and why it matters). Visibility work should serve your wellbeing, not undermine it. Clearly stating your niche and building consistency are healthy practices.

title: Building Your Personal Brand as a Wisdom Source - Quiz
